verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after the uproar was ceased, Paul called unto him the disciples, and embraced them, and departed for to go into Macedonia.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There was need of much comforting after that uproar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Accordingly, having done this, he goes into Macedonia, and then into Greece.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, it says, “when he had gone over those parts, and had given them much exhortation, he came into Greece, and there abode three months.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when the Jews laid wait for him, as he was about to sail into Syria, he purposed to return through Macedonia.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again he is persecuted by the Jews, and goes into Macedonia.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there accompanied him into Asia Sopater of Berea; and of the Thessalonians, Aristarchus and Secundus; and Gaius of Derbe, and Timotheus; and of Asia, Tychicus and Trophimus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These going before tarried for us at Troas.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how does he call Timothy a man “of Thessalonica?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is not his meaning, but, “Of Thessalonians, Aristarchus and Secundus and Gaius: of Derbe, Timothy,” etc., these, he says, went before him to Troas, preparing the way for him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And we sailed away from Philippi after the days of unleavened bread, and came unto them to Troas in five days; where we abode seven days.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it seems to me that he made a point of keeping the feasts in the large cities.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “From Philippi,” where the affair of the prison had taken place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was his third coming into Macedonia, and it is a high testimony that he bears to the Philippians, which is the reason why he makes some stay there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And upon the day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them, ready to depart on the morrow; and continued his speech until midnight.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was then the (season between Easter and) Pentecost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how everything was subordinate to the preaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was also, it says, the Lord’s day.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not even during night-time was he silent, nay he discoursed the rather then, because of stillness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how he both made a long discourse, and beyond the time of supper itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the Devil disturbed the feast—not that he prevailed, however—by plunging the hearer in sleep, and causing him to fall down.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” it says, “there were many lights in the upper chamber, where they were gathered together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And there sat in a window a certain young man named Eutychus, being fallen into a deep sleep: and as Paul was long preaching, he sunk down with sleep, and fell down from the third loft, and was taken up dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul went down, and fell on him, and embracing him, said, Trouble not yourselves; for his life is in him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he therefore was come up again, and had broken bread, and eaten, and talked a long while, even till break of day, so he departed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they brought the young man alive, and were not a little comforted.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe, I pray you, the theatre, how crowded it was: and the miracle, what it was.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He was sitting in a window,” at dead of night.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was their eagerness to hear him!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us take shame to ourselves!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Aye, but a Paul” say you, “was discoursing then.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, and Paul discourses now, or rather not Paul, either then or now, but Christ, and yet none cares to hear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No window in the case now, no importunity of hunger, or sleep, and yet we do not care to hear: no crowding in a narrow space here, nor any other such comfort.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the wonderful circumstance is, that though he was a youth, he was not listless and indifferent; and though (he felt himself) weighed down by sleep, he did not go away, nor yet fear the danger of falling down.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not from listlessness that he slumbered, but from necessity of nature.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe, I beseech you, so fervent was their zeal, that they even assembled in a third loft: for they had not a Church yet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Trouble not yourselves,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He said not, “He shall come to life again, for I will raise him up:” but mark the unassuming way in which he comforts them: “for his life,” says he, “is in him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he was come up again, and had broken bread, and eaten.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This thing cut short the discourse; it did no harm, however.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When he had eaten,” it says, “and discoursed a long while, even till break of day, so he departed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the frugality of the supper?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe how they passed the whole night?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such were their meals, that the hearers came away sober, and fit for hearing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But we, in what do we differ from dogs?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark what a difference (between us and those men)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they brought the young man alive, and,” it says, “were not a little comforted,” both because they received him back alive, and because a miracle had been wrought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And we went before to ship, and sailed unto Thasos, there intending to take in Paul: for so had he appointed, minding himself to go afoot.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We often find Paul parting from the disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For behold again, he himself goes afoot: giving them the easier way, and himself choosing the more painful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He went afoot, both that he might arrange many matters, and by way of training them to bear a parting from him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had joined us at Thasos, having taken him on board, we came to Mytilene; and having sailed thence on the morrow, we come over against Chios”—then they pass the island—“and on the next day we touched at Samos, and having stopped at Trogylium, on the following day we came to Miletus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For Paul had determined to sail by Ephesus, because he would not spend the time in Asia: for he hasted, if it were possible for him, to be in Jerusalem the day of Pentecost.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why this haste?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not for the sake of the feast, but of the multitude.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the same time, by this he conciliated the Jews, as being one that did honor the feasts, wishing to gain even his adversaries: at the same time also he delivers the word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Accordingly, see what great gain accrued, from all being present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that the interests of the people of Ephesus might not be neglected on that account, he managed for this in a different way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having embraced them,” it says, “he departed for to go into Macedonia.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By this again he refreshed them (ἀνεκτήσατο), giving them much consolation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having exhorted” the Macedonians, “with much discourse, he came into Greece.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how we everywhere find him accomplishing all by means of preaching, not by miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And we, sailed,” etc. The writer constantly shows him to us as hasting to get to Syria; and the reason of it was the Church, and Jerusalem, but still he restrained his desire, so as to set all right in those parts also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet Troas is not a large place: why then do they pass seven days in it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps it was large as regarded the number of believers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And after he had passed seven days there, on the following day he spent the night in teaching: so hard did he find it to tear himself away from them, and they from him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when we came together” it says, “to break bread.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the very time (of breaking bread) the discourse having taken its commencement,* extended: as representing that they were hungry, and it was not unseasonable: for the principal object (which brought them together) was not teaching, but they came together “to break bread;” discourse however having come up, he prolonged the teaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how all partook also at Paul’s table.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me, that he discoursed while even sitting at table, teaching us to consider all other things as subordinate to this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Picture to yourselves, I beseech you, that house with its lights, with its crowd, with Paul in the midst, discoursing, with even the windows occupied by many: what a thing it was to see, and to hear that trumpet, and behold that gracious countenance!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why did he discourse during night time?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since “he was about to depart,” it says, and was to see them no more: though this indeed he does not tell them, they being too weak (to bear it), but he did tell it to the others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the same time too the miracle which took place would make them evermore to remember that evening; so that the fall turned out to the advantage of the teacher.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great was the delight of the hearers, and even when interrupted it was the more increased.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That (young man) was to rebuke all that are careless (of the word), he whose death was caused by nothing else than this, that he wished to hear Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And we went before to ship,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore does the writer say where they came, and where they went to?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To show in the first place that he was making the voyage more leisurely—and this upon human grounds—and sailing past (some): also (for the same reason he tells) where he made a stay, and what parts he sailed past; (namely,) “that he might not have to spend the time in Asia.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since had he come there, he could not have sailed by; he did not like to pain those who would have begged him to remain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For he hasted,” it says, “if it were possible for him to keep the day of Pentecost in Jerusalem:” and (this) was not possible (if he stayed).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, how he is also moved like other men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For therefore it is that all this is done, that we may not fancy that he was above human nature: (therefore) you see him desiring (something), and hasting, and in many instances not obtaining (his object): for those great and holy men were partakers of the same nature with us; it was in the will and purpose that they differed, and so it was that also they attracted upon themselves the great grace they did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, for instance, how many things they order by an economy of their own.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That we give not offence” (2 Cor. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 3) to those who wish (to take offence), and, “That our ministry be not blamed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold, both an irreproachable life and on the other hand condescension.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is (indeed to be) called economy, to the (very) summit and height (of it).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he that went beyond the commandments of Christ, was on the other hand more humble than all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I am made all things to all men,” he says, “that I might gain all.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He cast himself also upon dangers, as he says in another place; “In much patience, in afflictions, in necessities, in distresses, in stripes, in imprisonments.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And great was his love for Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if there be not this, all else is superfluous, both the economy (of condescending accommodation), and the irreproachable life, and the exposing himself to dangers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who is weak,” he says, “and I am not weak?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who is offended, and I burn not?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These words let us imitate, and let us cast ourselves upon dangers for our brethren’s sake.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whether it be fire, or the sword, cast thyself on it, beloved, that thou mayest rescue (him that is) thy member: cast thyself, be not afraid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou art a disciple of Christ, Who laid down His life for His brethren: a fellow-disciple with Paul, who chose to suffer numberless ills for his enemies, for men that were warring against him; be thou filled with zeal, imitate Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He saw one suffering wrong, and avenged him; he despised royal luxury, and for the sake of those who were afflicted he became a fugitive, a wanderer, lonely and deserted; he passed his days in a foreign land; and yet he blamed not himself, nor said, “What is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I despised royalty, with all that honor and glory: I chose to avenge those who were wronged, and God hath overlooked me: and not only hath He not brought me back to my former honor, but even forty years am I passing in a foreign land.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Truly, handsomely have I received my wages, have I not!”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But nothing of the kind did he say or think.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So also do thou: be it that thou suffer any evil for doing good, be it that (thou have to wait) a long time, be not thou offended, be not discomposed: God will of a surety give thee thy reward.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The more the recompense is delayed, the more is the interest of it increased.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us have a soul apt to sympathize, let us have a heart that knows how to feel with others in their sorrows: no unmerciful temper (ὠμόν), no inhumanity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though thou be able to confer no relief, yet weep thou, groan, grieve over what has happened: even this is not to no purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If it behooves us to feel for those who are justly punished by God, much more for those who suffer unjustly at the hands of men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (They of) “Ænan,” it saith, “came not forth to mourn for the house which was near her” (Micah i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11): they shall receive pain, “in return for that they built for derision.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, Ezekiel makes this an accusation against them, that they did not grieve for (the afflicted).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sayest thou, O Prophet?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God punisheth, and shall I grieve for those that He is punishing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yea verily: for God Himself that punisheth wisheth this: since neither does He Himself wish to punish, nay, even Himself grieves when punishing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then be not thou glad at it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You will say, “If they are justly punished, we ought not to grieve.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, the thing we ought to grieve for is this—that they were found worthy of punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, when thou seest thy son undergoing cautery or the knife, dost thou not grieve? and sayest thou not to thyself, “What is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is for health this cutting, to quicken his recovery; it is for his deliverance, this burning?” but for all that, when thou hearest him crying out, and not able to bear the pain, thou grievest, and the hope of health being restored is not enough to carry off the shock to nature.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So also in the case of these, though it be in order to their health that they are punished, nevertheless let us show a brotherly feeling, a fatherly disposition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They are cuttings and cauteries, the punishments sent by God: but it is for this we ought to weep, that they were sick, that they needed such a mode of cure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If it be for crowns that any suffer these things, then grieve not; for instance, as Paul, as Peter suffered: but when it is for punishment that one suffers justice, then weep, then groan.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was the part the prophets acted; thus one of them said, “Ah!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lord, dost thou destroy the residue of Israel?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We see men-slayers, wicked men, suffering punishment, and we are distressed, and grieve for them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not be philosophical beyond measure: let us show ourselves pitiful, that we may be pitied; there is nothing equal to this beautiful trait: nothing so marks to us the stamp of human nature as the showing pity, as the being kind to our fellow-men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, therefore do the laws consign to public executioners the whole business of punishment: having compelled the judge to punish so far as to pronounce the sentence, thereafter they call forth those to perform the act itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So true is it, that though it be justly done, it is not the part of a generous (φιλοσόφου) soul to inflict punishment, but it requires another sort of person for this: since even God punishes not by His own hand, but by means of the angels.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are they then executioners, the angels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God forbid: I say not this, but they are avenging powers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When Sodom was destroyed, the whole was done by them as the instruments: when the judgments in Egypt were inflicted, it was through them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “He sent,” it says, “evil angels among them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Psalm lxxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when there is need of saying, God does this by Himself: thus, He sent the Son:—(b) but, “He that receiveth you, receiveth Me, and he that receiveth Me, receiveth Him that sent Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 40.) (a) And again He saith, “Then will I say unto the angels, Gather together them that do iniquity, and cast them into the furnace.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But concerning the just, not so. (c) And again, “Bind him hand and foot, and cast him into outer darkness.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how in that case His servants minister: but when the point is to do good, see Himself doing the good, Himself calling: “Come, ye blessed of My Father, inherit the Kingdom prepared for you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When the matter is, to converse with Abraham, then Himself comes to him: when it is, to depart to Sodom, He sends His servants, like a judge raising up those who are to punish.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things” (Matt. xxv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21); I (will make thee): but that other, not Himself, but His servants bind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Knowing these things, let us not rejoice over those who are suffering punishment, but even grieve: for these let us mourn, for these let us weep, that for this also we may receive a reward.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now, many rejoice even over those who suffer evil unjustly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not so, we: let us show all sympathy: that we also may have God vouchsafed us, through the grace and mercy of His only-begotten Son, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
